coredata.dc:title: Integrating synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles with carbon dots for selective detection of hemoglobin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yao
Zhang, Ming
Li, Linjun
Shea, Kenneth J.
Kang, Lingling
Meng, Zihui
Xue, Min
coredata.dc:description: Synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles (NPs) display high affinity to biomacromolecules target and have significant potential as protein capture agents for medical and biotechnological applications. In this study, we proposed a strategy to modify Carbon dots (CDs) with engineered NPs to address their detection specificity in complex biological sensing matrices. A library of NPs tagged CDs (NPs@CDs) incorporating hydrophobic and carboxylate groups that bound with high affinity to hemoglobin (Hb) was prepared by precipitation polymerization and screened based on the fluorescence quenching effect. NPs and CDs were integrated through noncovalent interaction, which was confirmed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analyses. The specificity of NPs@CDs was prominently attributed to the unique affinity between the screened NPs and Hb, compared with the contrast fluorescence quenching study by corresponding CDs to structural analogues and coexistence substances of Hb. Moreover, the Hb-induced fluorescence quenching process was dominated by a synergistic effect of internal filter effect (IFE) and static quenching proved by UV–vis absorption spectrometry and time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. The screened NPs@CDs showed strong recognition capability to Hb, and the fluorescence quenching rate was about 97 % which was 3.2 times that of CDs. A biosensing platform based on NPs@CDs was constructed, and the fluorescence signal of the nanoprobe decreased linearly as the concentration of Hb over the range of 0.2–80 μM L−1 with a detection limit of 7.5 nM L−1. The synergy between CDs and NPs resulted in high-speed real-time detection capability, high sensitivity, considerable specificity, low interference and good stability of this nanoprobe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrogel nanoparticles
Specificity
Hemoglobin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of hydrogel nanoparticles modified CDs
Characterization of CDs, NPs and NPs@ CDs
Affinity evaluation of NPs@CDs to Hb based on fluorescence quenching effect
Characterization of CDs, NPs and NPs@CDs
NPs@CDs preparation and screening based on the quenching efficiency to Hb
Specificity improvement by NPs displayed by the detection platform
Establishment of Hb detection approach by NPs@CDs nanoprobe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Min Xue received her master degree from Beijing Institute of Technology and then obtained her Ph.D. degree from University of Fukui. She was supported by Chinese Scholarship Council and worked in the research group of Prof. Kenneth J. Shea from UC Irvine as a visiting scholar in 2012.Currently, she is an associate professor of Beijing Institute of Technology and focuses on chromatography technology and functional polymer materials.
This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874009).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and characterization of CDs, NPs and NPs@CDs. (a) TEM image of as-prepared CDs, the insets corresponding to the size-distribution histogram and their HRTEM image; SEM images of (b) as-prepared NPs and (c) as-prepared nanoprobe unit NPs@CDs (the insert graph as HRTEM image of CDs on NPs’ surface); (d) The hydrodynamic size distribution of NPs and NPs@CDs measured by DLS; (e) FTIR spectra of CDs, NPs and NPs@CDs; (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (I) and NPs@CDs (II), the insets corresponding to the photographs of CDs (left) and NPs@CDs (right) in aqueous solution under UV light; Fluorescence spectra of (g) CDs and (h) NPs@CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
Sensing selectivity of NPs@CDs toward Hb. The concentration of the analytes was 0.3 mg mL−1.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrogram of Hb (I) and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NPs@CDs (II, III); (b) Fluorescence decay proﬁles of NPs@CDs with and without Hb; (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Hb, NPs@CDs, NPs@CDs+Hb and NPs@CDs-Hb; (d) Schematic diagram of the preparation of the nanoprobe NPs@CDs and possible recognition mechanism to Hb. The concentration of Hb and NPs@CDs in the spectrum analysis was 0.3 mg mL−1 and 0.125 mg mL−1, respectively.

Optimization and establishment of detection conditions including pH value (a) and ion strength (b); (c) The effect of concentration of NPs@CDs to quenching efficiency; (d) The effect of incubation time of the test solution to quenching efficiency; (e) The effect of temperature to the quenching efficiency of NPs@CDs; (f) Hydrodynamic diameter of NPs@CDs at different temperatures; (g) Fluorescence spectra of NPs@CDs with different concentrations of Hb; (h) Linear relationship between (I0 − I)/I0 and Hb concentration.
Feed ratio of functional monomers and CDs of NPs@CDs and their characterization parameters.
Comparison of the proposed method with previously reported analytical techniques.
Determination of Hb in human plasma with NPs@CDs nanoprobe.

head.ce:title: Integrating synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles with carbon dots for selective detection of hemoglobin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of California, Irvine (UCI), Irvine, CA 92697-2025, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, University of California, Irvine (UCI)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of California, Irvine (UCI), Irvine, California 92697-2025, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles (NPs) display high affinity to biomacromolecules target and have significant potential as protein capture agents for medical and biotechnological applications. In this study, we proposed a strategy to modify Carbon dots (CDs) with engineered NPs to address their detection specificity in complex biological sensing matrices. A library of NPs tagged CDs (NPs@CDs) incorporating hydrophobic and carboxylate groups that bound with high affinity to hemoglobin (Hb) was prepared by precipitation polymerization and screened based on the fluorescence quenching effect. NPs and CDs were integrated through noncovalent interaction, which was confirmed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analyses. The specificity of NPs@CDs was prominently attributed to the unique affinity between the screened NPs and Hb, compared with the contrast fluorescence quenching study by corresponding CDs to structural analogues and coexistence substances of Hb. Moreover, the Hb-induced fluorescence quenching process was dominated by a synergistic effect of internal filter effect (IFE) and static quenching proved by UV–vis absorption spectrometry and time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. The screened NPs@CDs showed strong recognition capability to Hb, and the fluorescence quenching rate was about 97 % which was 3.2 times that of CDs. A biosensing platform based on NPs@CDs was constructed, and the fluorescence signal of the nanoprobe decreased linearly as the concentration of Hb over the range of 0.2–80 μM L−1 with a detection limit of 7.5 nM L−1. The synergy between CDs and NPs resulted in high-speed real-time detection capability, high sensitivity, considerable specificity, low interference and good stability of this nanoprobe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescent nano-materials and considered as a biofriendly alternative to conventional organic dye and quantum dots [1,2]. CDs have stable photoluminescence, adjustable excitation and emission wavelengths. Moreover, their surface functionality can be readily modified [3]. Recently, CDs have attracted attention in biological labeling, photocatalysis, biomedicine imaging and sensing owing to their photophysical properties and low toxicity [4–6]. However, their fluorescence analytical application was compromised in some complex matrix attributed to the unsatisfied anti-interference ability and selectivity. Both doping with heteroatom or metal ions and grafting nanomaterials were proposed to improve their specificity to the target analytes [7,8]. For example, nitrogen doped carbon dots from urea and glucose were used as a dual sensor for tetracycline, which selective fluorescence quenching was evaluated in the presence of analogues with double concentration [9]. Another dual-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor fabricated via self-assembly of CDs–loaded SiO2 nanoparticles showed significant sensitivity and wide detection range in tetracycline monitoring [10]. Moreover, due to the remarkable selectivity to target molecules, molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) were successfully integrated with CDs to enhance their specificity [11]. CDs modified with N-acetylneuraminic acid and glucuronic acid imprinted polymers could selectively target and image HepG-2 (human hepatoma carcinoma) and MCF-7 human breast cancer cells [12].

Likewise, hydrogel nanoparticles (NPs) could be another promising stuff to enhance the selectivity of CDs, which recently were developed as synthetic affinity ligands or antibodies. They possess excellent specificity and affinity to proteins and peptides, together with advantages of biocompatibility, chemical and mechanical robustness, easy-to-obtain raw materials, and non-biological synthetic methods [13]. These three dimensionally crosslinked hydrophilic nano-sized polymers are comparable with antibody and can be tuned with biological specificity based upon their chemical composition. A series of NPs, which exhibited both specificity and affinity to proteins and peptides including IgG, histones, melittin, VEGF165 and so on, have been designed [14–16]. In a number of cases, they exhibited a potential advantage over antibodies for complex and multifunctional proteins at specific domains and also inhibited their function both in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, CDs tagged by engineered NPs were expected for biomacromolecules specific detection due to their high binding capacity, broad-spectrum targeting and biocompatibility.

In this study, we proposed a strategy tagging NPs to CDs for Hemoglobin (Hb) detection, since the facile, accurate and rapid measurement of Hb in blood samples is always significant for clinical medicine. Hb level out of the normal range may predict the risk of anemia, leukemia, heart diseases and certain tumors [17]. The NPs tagged CDs (NPs@CDs) were performed as a fluorescent probe which specificity was guaranteed through the affinity screening study of NPs. NPs@CDs with uniform size were prepared by radical precipitation polymerization method in aqueous solution with the optimized content of CDs and appropriate formula of NPs. Comparing with CDs, this probe platform exhibited an extremely higher selective detection of Hb, especially in analogues recognition and determination. Their application for the assay of Hb in human blood samples was also performed.

Acrylic acid (AAc, purity 99 %), N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm, purity 99 %, recrystallized in n-hexane before use), N,N′-methylene-bis(acrylamide) (BIS, purity 98 %, recrystallized in ethanol), N-tert-butylacrylamide (TBAm, purity 97 %), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, analytical grade) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ammonium persulfate (APS, analytical grade), Citric acid monohydrate (CA·H2O, analytical pure) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Plant. Ethylene diamine (EDA, analytical pure), potassium bromide (KBr, purity 99 %), glycine, histidine，dopamine, glucose were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Hemoglobin (Hb, purity 98 %), Human Serum Albumin (HSA, purity 98 %), Cytochrome C, Lysozyme (≥20000 U mg−1) were purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Human blood was provided from Shanghai Hengyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Carbon Dots (CDs) were synthesized by a bottom-up microwave assisted hydrothermal method [18]. After 0.4203 g citric acid and 0.252 g ethyl enediamine were fully dissolved in 80 mL ultrapure water, the solution was transferred to poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 160 ºC for 4 h. Then, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 14,000 r/min for 20 min and filtered through 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove the agglomerated particles after cooling down to the room temperature. Finally, the filtrate was puriﬁed by dialyzing against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500) for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hydrogel nanoparticles modified CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogel Nanoparticles modified CDs (NPs@CDs) were synthesized by precipitation polymerization method. Different feed ratios of N-t-butyl acrylamide (TBAm, X mol%), acrylic acid (AAc, Y mol%) and N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm, 98-X-Y mol%) together with N, N’-methylene-bis(acrylamide) (BIS, 2 mol%), finally resulted in a total monomer concentration of 65 mM, were dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water consisting of quantitative obtained CDs solution. Besides, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was presented as an anionic surfactant during the polymerization process. After the above-mentioned substances were mixed in a three-necked, round-bottomed flask, the nitrogen was bubbled through the reaction mixtures for 30 min to remove the air existing in the reaction system. Then the reaction solution was heated to 65 ºC, followed by the addition of 30 mg ammonium persulfate (APS) as the initiator. The whole process lasted 3 h under 65 ºC and a nitrogen atmosphere. Finally, the obtained solution was puriﬁed by dialysis against an excess amount of pure water for four days. Corresponding hydrogel Nanoparticles were synthesized in the same process without CDs solution presence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, NPs and NPs@ CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The density measurement of CDs, NPs and NPs@CDs was performed with freeze-drying method. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements were completed by using Zetasizer Nano-ZS Dynamic Light Scattering Laser Particle Sizer (Malvern, UK). UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained using a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Pgeneral, China) at room temperature. Fluorescent measurements were carried out by using F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), which were measured in quartz cuvettes under the condition that the width of the excitation and emission slits was 2.5 nm and 5.0 nm, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on JEM-2100 High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 V. The sample solutions of CDs and NPs@CDs were carefully dripped onto the 300-mesh copper grid coated with a lacy carbon film, and the solvent was evaporated at ambient temperature and pressure. Fourier Transform Infra Red (FTIR) spectra of the samples were collected on a Thermo IS5 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co., Ltd.). Samples were scanned 128 times at a resolution 2 cm−1, over the range of 4000 ∼ 400 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Affinity evaluation of NPs@CDs to Hb based on fluorescence quenching effect
ce:section.ce:para:
The obtained NPs@CDs aqueous solution was diluted to 1.0 mg mL−1 based on the density value, 100 μL of which was diluted with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH 5.5) and mixed with quantitative Hb aqueous solution, finally resulted in a total volume of 1.0 mL. After an incubation period of 3 min, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured by the fluorescence spectrometer. The ﬂuorescence spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength of 390 nm, and the band-slits of excitation and emission were set as 5.0 nm. The sensitivity was ﬁxed based on height and the response time was set as 0.5 s. The emission spectra were recorded from 400 to 750 nm, and the ﬂuorescence intensity change of NPs@CDs at 554 nm was employed in the quantitative analysis of Hb, which was expressed by the quenching rate according to Eq. 1. The experiments were parallelly repeated for three times and the average data was calculated.
(1)
The quenching rate
%
=
( I
0
− I ) /
I
0
× 100 %
where I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of NPs@CDs in the absence and presence of Hb, respectively.

Human blood samples were placed in anticoagulation tubes and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The lower layer of the serum was collected and then the red blood cell lysis solution was added (volume ratio 1:1). The mixture solution was packed into 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes followed by oscillation for 30 min. After storing at 4 °C overnight, the solution was again centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Hb in the supernatant was determined by the fluorescent probe NPs@CDs after diluting 2000-fold with PBS solution (pH 5.5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, NPs and NPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid and its derivants are common carbon sources of CDs for bioimaging and sensing although their arbitrary applications are limited by the non-specific fluorescence especially in the complicate matrix [19]. Herein, a series of synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles (NPs) participated by CDs were prepared and screened based on their binding affinity to Hb. The morphology of CDs was confirmed by TEM, whereupon the polymer-like CDs were moulded followed by the final formation of CDs through the carbonization. The combination of CDs and NPs was validated by Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and FTIR spectroscopy through the particles size increase and the functional group analysis.

CDs were uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation and their particle diameters were about 2 ∼ 5 nm proved by TEM ( Fig. 1a), which was well consistent with the reported literature [20]. Both the average diameter as around 3.2 nm and the corresponding nanoparticle size distribution histogram was obtained by counting one hundred particles as shown in the insert. Moreover, the structure of CDs with legible lattice fringes was depicted by the HRTEM image in another inset, indicating the highly crystalline structure. A line cutting (white dotted line in the insert) was further performed to extract the lattice parameter of ∼0.19 nm representing the lattice fringe of graphene. Therefore, their graphitic nature was proved by their crystalline structure and lattice spacing distance simultaneously. The spherical NPs and NPs@CDs were well dispersed with average diameter of around 60 ± 5 nm and 70 ± 5 nm by SEM, respectively (Figs. 1b, 1c), which were about 10 nm lower than those obtained from DLS due to the hydrated layer being destroyed in SEM detection process (Fig. 1d). Compared with the individual NPs, the increase in dimension of the assembled nanoprobe NPs@CDs was attributed to the combination of CDs and NPs. Abundant CDs particles were observed on the surface of NPs@CDs which resulted in the 2 ∼ 6 nm increase of the diameter. The distinct and uniform lattice fringes with inter-planar spacing distance about 0.2 nm of the prepared materials were presented in the inset of Fig. 1c, which further proved the existence of CDs on the NPs’ surface. Compared with the hydrodynamic size of NPs, there was overall 6 nm size increase of NPs@CDs revealed by DLS data, which was exactly consistent with the SEM results.

Chemical composition of CDs, NPs and NPs@CDs were ascertained by FT-IR, which further validated the combination of CDs and NPs (Fig. 1e). Overlapping O-H/N-H stretching bands were detected at 3400 cm−1and 3250 cm−1, whereas peaks at 2893 cm−1 and 672 cm−1 corresponded to C-H stretching and bending vibrations, which indicated the presence of N element on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, peak at 1649 cm−1 corresponded to CO or CC stretch of carbon backbone of CDs. It is proved that CDs were functionalized with hydroxyl, carboxylic, carbonyl and amino groups, derived from organic micromolecule citric acid and ethyl-enediamine under microwave-hydrothermal conditions. The similar absorption peaks were found in both NPs and NPs@CDs. Peaks in the range of 2971–3330 cm−1 ascribed to a broad N-H stretching vibration and C-H stretching vibration peak; peaks at 1653 and 1533 cm−1 owing to CO and N-H were from the amido bond, whereas peak at 1224 cm−1 assigned to the C-N of secondary amide. The consistency of the infrared spectrum of NPs@CDs and unadulterated NPs indicated that the chemical structure of NPs was remained after CDs being introduced.

The fluorescent behavior of the synthesized nanoprobe NPs@CDs was investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. NPs@CDs showed a broad UV peak at about 205 nm and a high-energy peak at 316 nm, which were assigned to π−π* transitions of sp2-hybridized carbon and n−π* transitions at the edge of the carbon lattice, respectively. Compared with which of CDs, an observable blue shift was observed attributed to the noncovalent interactions such as hydrogen-bonding when CDs were conjugated to the NPs. CDs and NPs@CDs aqueous solutions showed bright blue and turquoise under UV light (254 nm), respectively (Fig. 1f). Fluorescence spectrum of CDs over different excitation wavelengths was shown in Fig. 1g, where a strong excitation-independent emission at about 450 nm was observed. However, excitation-dependent emission of NPs@CDs was recognized owing to the different surface energy traps when CDs were embedded in NPs (Fig. 1h). An obvious red-shift of the emission peak of the nanoprobe NPs@CDs caused by the increasing λ
ex was observed. The most intensive fluorescence occurred at 554 nm which was excited at λ
ex 390 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of NPs@CDs decreased comparing with that of CDs, which may be caused by the limited binding sites on the NPs’ surface and led to the combination reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NPs@CDs preparation and screening based on the quenching efficiency to Hb
ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles have been proved practicable binding to speciﬁc biomacromolecules by a combination of electrostatic, hydrogen-bonding, van der Waals, and/or hydrophobic interactions [21]. Therefore, the synthesis formula of NPs@CDs was the decisive factor to the specific binding affinity between NPs@CDs and Hb. NPs based on the different contents of hydrophobic and electrophilic monomers were grafted to CDs. The feed ratio of TBAm and AAc contributing the hydrophobicity and the electrophilicity was optimized based on the decrement of fluorescence intensity. As the exclusive fluorescent source of this nanoprobe, the addition content of CDs was critical to the detection. Therefore, a small combinatorial library of multifunctional hydrogel complex NPs@CDs was synthesized using three-factor and four-level orthogonal experiment (L1643) to optimize the chemical composition of the nanoprobe, where the proportion of functional monomers, content of CDs and the obtained characterizes of NPs@CDs such as size, the index of dispersion (PDI), zeta potential and quenching efficiency were displayed in Table 1.

As shown in Table 1, the binding capacity of NPs@CDs to Hb increased firstly and decreased subsequently with the feed ratio of hydrophobic monomer TBAm, and the strongest fluorescence intensity signal was obtained when NPs contained TBAm (30 %) incorporating AAc groups. It is known that the target protein, hemoglobin, is a tetrameric molecule composed of two α-like and two β-like chains where the O2-binding heme group existing in each chain is a ferrous iron containing protoporphyrin ring buried in a hydrophobic pocket. The tight binding could be in part attributed to the hydrophobic interaction between NPs@CDs and the methyl and ethyl groups of the heme. Furthermore, the binding capacity was enhanced when the proportion of AAc monomer increased, which provided the electrostatic interactions between negatively charged NPs@CDs and the positively charged Hb. However, the optimal proportion of AAc was restricted at 5 % since the isoelectric point (PI) of Hb was around 6.81 which presented weak electropoisivity in the aqueous solution of pH 5.5.

The statistical analysis of the orthogonal experimental results was summarized, where k
1
, k
2
, k
3,
k
4 were the average indexes of different experimental factors at the four levels of 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively; R was the range of k
1
, k
2,
k
3 and k
4 , which reflected the influence of this factor on the average index. It suggested that the proportion of AAc and TBAm in NPs@CDs was quite vital to the quenching rate attributed to the affinity, and both the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions contributed to the strong binding of NPs@CDs to the target. Furthermore, the quenching rate was greatly affected by the monomer AAc according to the range value R. The almost equal average indexes at the four levels of content of CDs and the minimum range indicated their weak contribution to the affinity. These results indicated that the specificity of the nanoprobe to Hb was mainly regulated by the monomer segments and CDs were the source of fluorescence.

Therefore, the preparation formulation of NPs@CDs with complimentary binding sites to Hb was optimized which feed ratio of AAc, TBAm, NIPAm and BIS was 5/30/63/2 (mol%) together with 15 mg as-prepared CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity improvement by NPs displayed by the detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: Both CDs and NPs@CDs were applied on several kinds of inorganic ions, amino acids and proteins which may co-exist with Hb in serum ( Fig. 2). A significant fluorescence quenching was observed when CDs were presented in Cu2+, Fe2+, cytochrome C (Cyt C) and Hb solution. This attributed to the chelate formation between amino and carboxyl groups on the CDs’ surface and Cu2+/Fe2+ independence or existing in Cyt C and Hb, which agreed with the reported literature [22]. However, the quenching rate of Hb with the same content by NPs@CDs was greatly increased to 97 % (about 3.2 times that of CDs) while that of Cu2+, Fe2+ and Cyt C dropped over 90 %.

The specificity should be ascribed to the unique affinity between the screened NPs and Hb. NPs modified on CDs were capable of effective recognizing and combining with Hb owing to the multiple non-covalent interactions including electrostatic interaction, hydrophobic interaction and van der Waals. On the contrary, the participation of NPs dramatically reduced this composite probe’s recognition capability of other analysts such as Cu2+, Fe2+ and Cyt C. Therefore, the introduction of NPs retained the fluorescence quenching property of CDs to Hb while their specificity was significantly improved.

The quenching mechanism of NPs@CDs was studied by a series of optical tests. As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–vis absorption of Hb overlapped with the excitation spectrum of NPs@CDs, suggesting that Hb could shield the excitation of NPs@CDs. Therefore, the quenching mechanism between NPs@CDs and Hb may be involved in inter filter effect (IFE), which was confirmed by the fluorescence lifetimes being approximately identical and distinguished as 8.69 ns and 8.62 ns in the presence and absence of Hb, respectively (Fig. 3b) [23,24]. Meanwhile, the negligible change of fluorescence decay lifetime with and without the presence of the quencher suggested the static quenching effect of Hb toward NPs@CDs [25]. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption of NPs@CDs and Hb mixture solution (NPs@CDs-Hb was not the simple superposition of that of NPs@CDs and Hb within the experimental error (Fig. 3c), but the absorption intensity of NPs@CDs-Hb showed a tiny decrease at the maximum absorption wavelength of Hb (403 nm) compared with the sum of NPs@CDs and Hb (NPs@CDs+Hb). Therefore, the ground state chelate complex with hypochromic effect was presented which further proved that the static quenching mechanism also goes for NPs@CDs.

Generally, the monodispersed NPs were constructed in aqueous solution by co-monomers AAc, TBAm and NIPAm connecting with each other and forming co-polymer chains in the presence of cross-linker BIS, where numerous groups such as carboxyl, amino and carbonyl existed. The intermolecular hydrogen bonds together with electrostatic attraction were developed between NPs and CDs in the integrated nanoprobe as shown in Fig. 3d. The engineered and screened NPs endowed the specific affinity to the target protein Hb by multiple noncovalent interactions dominated by electrostatic interaction and hydrophobic interaction. Besides, the spherical structure, the feature of high-dispersion and flexibility of hydrogel endowed the close integration of NPs@CDs and Hb by non-covalent combination. The abundant amino and carboxyl groups on their surface endowed NPs@CDs the chelating ability, which could interact with ferrous iron existing in heme group of Hb as an iron chelator. Meanwhile, the formation of the chelate complex changed the trajectory of electron transition from ground state to excited state or the way of electron return to ground state after transition, leading to significant fluorescence quenching of NPs@CDs [26]. Therefore, the unique specificity to Hb by NPs@CDs was realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of Hb detection approach by NPs@CDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the specificity, convenience and sensitivity of this ﬂuorescent nanoprobe, NPs@CDs was performed on Hb detection.

The efficiency of fluorescence quenching of NPs@CDs to Hb was studied at different pH values since hydrogen ion in the test solution is aggressively involved in the electrostatic interactions between NPs@CDs and Hb. It was found that the fluorescence quenching rate gradually increased when the pH value was adjusted from 4.0 to 5.5, but significantly decreased while the pH value was above 5.5 ( Fig. 4a). This may be attributed to the electrostatic attraction between NPs@CDs and Hb, which played the dominant role when pH value of the test solution was far below the isoelectric point of Hb but weakened along with the increase of pH value [27]. So, Hb tended to combine with the negative charged NPs@CDs in acidic solution. The quenching effect of NPs@CDs was basically unchanged in NaCl concentration range of 0–1.5 M as shown in Fig. 4b. NPs@CDs was stable with good salt resistance and their electrostatic balance was unsusceptible. Therefore, the robust fluorescence property of NPs@CDs regardless of ionic strength may guarantee their convenient application in real biological sample [28].

The amount of NPs@CDs composites was optimized in case low sensitivity was induced by the self-absorption quenching when excess NPs@CDs presented. Thus, NPs@CDs solutions in a range of 0.025 mg mL−1 to 1.0 mg mL−1 were organized in 0.3 mg mL−1 Hb solution. The fluorescence responses were recorded after 5 min incubation. As shown in Fig. 4c, 0.125 mg mL−1 was decided while NPs@CDs aggregated and the electrostatic balance may be disturbed with the extra addition. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at different incubation time as shown in Fig. 4d. The response time was about 3 min after the addition of Hb and then remained constantly for more than 60 min, indicating this nanoprobe had the alike excellent stability and rapid response ability to Hb. The rapid response time should be benefited from the uniformly dispersed CDs on NPs surface.

The temperature influence on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of NPs@CDs to Hb was studied based on Stern-Volmer fitting (Eq. (2)) [29].
(2)
I
0
I
= 1 +
K
SV
×
Q
where I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of Hb; [Q] is the analytical concentration of Hb; K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, which can be used as a binding constant if a static quenching mechanism.

We projected the fluorescence intensities ratio of NPs@CDs 6 before and after the addition of Hb to a line sketched while the concentration of Hb and (I0/I)-1 was horizontal and vertical coordinates, respectively (Fig. 4e). The slope of the standard curves (K
SV ) increased when the temperature rosed from 5 °C to 15 °C, which means the quenching efficiency of Hb to NPs@CDs increased. The reason came from the shrinkage of NPs@CDs along with the temperature increasing (Fig. 4f), since 63 % of NIPAm was participated in NPs@CDs 6 which undergoes a volume-phase transition at a lower critical solution temperature (LCST). Therefore, the binding capacity of NPs@CDs was inferior and their quenching efficiency was bad while the temperature was below LCST. After the nanoparticles size maintained constant, K
SV reduced slightly while the matrices were heated up from 15 °C to 40 °C. This was in accordance with the characteristics of static quenching process that the quenching efficiency was diminished by the stability decline of the ground state complex along with temperature.

The ﬂuorescence emission spectra of NPs@CDs with addition of different concentrations of Hb were recorded under the optimum experimental conditions (Fig. 4g), where the ﬂuorescence intensity decline indicating high affinity between the nanoprobe and the Hb. Two linear relationships were obtained as (I
0
−I)/I
0 =3.204C
Hb +6.259 and (I
0
−I)/I
0 =0.297C
Hb +76.16, which correlation coefficient were 0.996 and 0.999, respectively. The corresponding concentration ranges were 0.2–24 μM L−1 and 24–80 μM L−1 (Fig. 4h). The limit of detection was 7.5 nM. Generally, the content level of Hb in blood lies in 130–180 mg mL–1 and 120–160 mg mL–1 for males and females, respectively [30]. So, the developed detection platform for Hb specific detection in human blood samples has potential clinical utilization.

In addition, we compared the analytical performance of NPs@CDs with the recently reported methods ( Table 2). The proposed probe exhibited a competitive sensitivity, excellent selectivity and rapid response capability toward Hb, which was mainly attributed to the integrating CDs with the engineered synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles.

The prepared fluorescent probe was utilized to detect Hb in human blood samples to demonstrate the applicability. As indicated in Table 3, the Hb concentrations in three blood samples of healthy adults were agreeing well with the normal Hb levels. Using the standard addition method, the recovery of 94.2 ∼ 103.8 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.9 ∼ 0.6 % were obtained. There is negligible interference to Hb determination from matrix components existing in the blood. Besides, a single drop of blood sample taken from patient’s fingertip instead of phlebotomize is enough for Hb content determination by this sensing platform owing to the outstanding detectability. The proposed method was simpler, more convenient and inexpensive compared with the immunoturbidimetric assay. All these advantages validated the reliability and the practicality of developed NPs@CDs based fluorescent probe for monitoring Hb in biological samples.

In conclusion, we proposed a novel strategy to enhance the specificity of CDs for fluorescent detection application based on grafting the engineered synthetic polymer nanoparticles with affinity and selectivity to the target protein. The integration of CDs and NPs was verified by TEM, DLS, and FTIR analysis. Meanwhile, the unique fluorescent property of CDs was perfectly remained and stable after being introduced NPs, which was proved by fluorometric testing. The quenching mechanism as a combination of IFE and static quenching mode to Hb was methodically realized by UV–vis absorption spectrometry and time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. Besides, the specificity was significantly enhanced after NPs tagged CDs especially being performed in analogues recognition and determination, which definitely attributed to the specific binding between Hb and the screened NPs. The fluorescence quenching rate of NPs@CDs by Hb increased 3.2 times compared with that of CDs while the metal ions like Cu2+, Fe2+ and hemoprotein such as Cyt C presented hardly quenching capability by this integrating nanoprobe. This result validated our initial goal of engineering the synthetic polymer NPs as modifying agent for biomacromolecules specific detection. Based on the unique specificity of NPs@CDs to Hb, a simple, stable, cost-effective, highly selective and rapid response detecting platform was constructed. The prepared nanoprobe showed good accuracy and anti-interference in determination of trace Hb in human plasma samples. All these results suggested that synthetic polymer NPs can be designed to have an intrinsic affinity to large biomacromolecules and further used as ligands to enhance specificity in application in sensing fields.

Linjun Li: Investigation, Data curation. Ming Zhang: Visualization. Yao Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zihui Meng: Project administration, Investigation. Min Xue: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Lingling Kang: Validation. Kenneth J. Shea: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874009).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yao Li received his bachelor’s degree in applied in chemistry from Inner Mongolia University in 2016. He is a master candidate at Beijing Institute of Technology and currently working on carbon dots and synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles.

Ming Zhang received his bachelor’s degree in applied in chemistry from Beijing Forestry University in 2023. He is a master candidate at Beijing Institute of Technology and currently working on carbon dots and synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles.

Linjun Li received her bachelor’s degree in applied in chemistry from Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology in 2016. She is a master candidate at Beijing Institute of Technology and currently working on synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles.

Kenneth J. Shea reccived his Ph.D. degree from University of Pennsylvania. He is currently a professor of University of California, Irvine, and was the dean of the Department of Chemistry from 2001 to 2004. His research areas include synthetic organic chemistry, polymer chemistry and materials science, etc.

Lingling Kang received her master’s degree in applied in chemistry from Beijing Institute of Technology in 2020. She is a doctoral candidate at Beijing Institute of Technology and currently working on carbon dots and synthetic hydrogel nanoparticles.

Zihui Meng received his Ph.D. degree in philosophy from Dalian Institute of Chemical& Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 1998. He then carried out his postdoctoral research at Beijing Institute of Pharmacology and Toxicology and Tokyo University of Agriculture & Technology. In 2005, he joined the Department of Applied Chemistry at Beijing Institute of Technology. His research areas include molecular imprinting, separation technology, chemical sensor and biosensor.

Min Xue received her master degree from Beijing Institute of Technology and then obtained her Ph.D. degree from University of Fukui. She was supported by Chinese Scholarship Council and worked in the research group of Prof. Kenneth J. Shea from UC Irvine as a visiting scholar in 2012.Currently, she is an associate professor of Beijing Institute of Technology and focuses on chromatography technology and functional polymer materials.
